text: The testing effect — the finding that retrieving information from memory strengthens it more than re-studying the same material — is closely related to desirable difficulty. Henry Roediger and Jeffrey Karpicke showed in 2006 that students who studied a passage once and were tested twice retained far more a week later than students who studied the passage three times. The act of testing is not merely assessment; it is itself a learning event. This reframes the role of examinations: not as a judgment imposed after learning, but as the most potent mechanism of learning available during the process.
question: What did Roediger and Karpicke's 2006 study demonstrate about the relative effectiveness of testing versus re-studying?

options.text: Testing is only more effective than re-studying when students receive immediate feedback on wrong answers
Students who studied once and were tested twice retained more a week later than those who studied three times
Re-studying is superior for procedural skills while testing benefits only factual recall
The testing effect is strongest when students are tested immediately after studying, not days later
